card_title: Bank says no fee. You still paid $300.

videos.video_title: How banks charge you nothing and still keep $300

variants.vo: I sent 10 grand to my supplier last month. The wire confirmation said zero fees. Zero. I screenshotted it. Then I checked Google for the foreign exchange rate, the actual mid-market rate banks use among themselves, at the time of the wire. The rate my bank used was 3 percent worse. That's 300 dollars they took without a single line item. No fee on paper. 300 dollars off in the rate. I called the bank. They said the rate is the rate. We switched to World Account the next week. Same wire, same supplier, the FX margin showed up on screen before I confirmed. Near-interbank. The 300 dollars stayed in the order. World Account is the multi-currency account that holds Chinese yuan natively. Link's in the bio.

Quick maths. You wire 10K to your supplier. Your bank says no fee. You believe them. Here's what they don't show you. The rate they used was 3 percent off the mid-market rate, the real rate Reuters publishes every minute. 3 percent of 10K is 300 dollars. Gone. No line item. No notification. Run that across 12 wires a year. That's 3,600 dollars sitting inside someone else's spreadsheet. World Account quotes the actual foreign exchange rate before you hit send. Margin is 0.3 to 0.5 percent, near-interbank. The 300 stays yours. Link's in the bio.

Everyone tells you to find a bank with no wire fees. Truth is, the wire fee was never the cost. Banks charge you in two places. One you see, that 25 dollars on the confirmation. One you don't, baked into the rate, 1 to 3 percent of the entire wire. On 10 grand, that hidden rate is 300 dollars. The visible fee is 25. Which one are you actually negotiating? Stop optimising the wrong number. World Account shows the foreign exchange rate before you confirm, so the math is honest. 0.3 to 0.5 percent margin, near the rate banks use among themselves. variants.vo: Three of us in the same WhatsApp group sent 50 grand to the same factory on the same day. We compared what landed. Operator A used a big bank, supplier got 49,250. Operator B used a regional bank, 49,700. I used World Account, 49,850. Same wire amount. Same supplier. Same Tuesday. 600 dollars spread across us three, and all of us paid no fee on paper. The difference was the foreign exchange rate each provider used. The bank rates were 1.5 and 0.6 percent off the mid-market. World Account was 0.3. World Account is a multi-currency account that holds Chinese yuan natively, so the conversion happens once at the real rate. Link's in the bio.

card_title: How a 2% bank-to-rail gap compounds over a year

variants.vo: I ran the numbers on my own sourcing last year. 12 wires to China. 50 grand each. I added up what I paid in foreign exchange margin, the spread between my bank's rate and the actual mid-market rate. 2 percent on average. On 600K of wires that's 12,000 dollars. Twelve grand I didn't see on any statement, didn't see on any confirmation. It just sat inside the rate. This year I'm on World Account. Margin's 0.3 to 0.5 percent. On the same 600K, that's around 2,400. I locked half the orders on Forwards too, so a chunk of the rate is fixed for 6 months ahead. The 9,500 dollar gap is just sitting back in the business. Link's in the bio.

card_title: Bank wire vs WorldFirst on the same $50K payment

videos.video_title: I sent $50K. My supplier got $48,500. Here is why.

variants.vo: I sent 50 grand to my factory last quarter. WhatsApp pings the next day. Supplier says, we got 48,560. I went back to the bank confirmation. Foreign exchange rate they used was 2.5 percent off the mid-market rate, the rate banks use among themselves. That's 1,250 dollars in the rate. Plus two SWIFT intermediary banks each shaved 30 to 40 dollars. Supplier was short 1,440. I wired again. New fee. New margin. Production delayed 4 days. The next order I sent through WorldFirst. Same 50K. Supplier got 49,850. 1,350 dollars more landed, no intermediaries, no second wire. WorldFirst runs 0.3 to 0.5 percent foreign exchange margin, near-interbank. Link's in the bio.

Quick maths. 50,000 dollar wire to your Chinese supplier. Bank route. Sending fee 50 dollars. Foreign exchange margin 2 percent, the spread baked into the rate, that's 1,000. Two SWIFT intermediary banks deduct 30 each from the wire, 60 total. Supplier gets 48,890. WorldFirst on the same 50K. 1688 World Pay routes via MYbank, Ant Group's bank, no SWIFT chain. Foreign exchange margin 0.4 percent, 200 dollars. Flat transfer cost, no intermediary deductions. Supplier gets 49,800. Difference, 910 dollars on a single wire. Same supplier. Same Tuesday. Link's in the bio.

The bank was taking double. The supplier didn't have my margin. The bank did. Switched to World Account. Margin's 0.3 to 0.5 percent. 18K of the 20K is back in my pocket. The supplier negotiation wasn't the lever. The rail was. Link's in the bio.

card_title: How to read FX margin off your wire confirmation

videos.video_title: Find the hidden FX margin in 30 seconds

variants.vo: Pulled out my last wire confirmation this morning. Did this exercise on camera. Step one, found the applied rate, 7.05. Step two, opened Google for the foreign exchange rate at the time of the wire, the mid-market rate, 7.22. Step three, did the math. 7.22 minus 7.05, divided by 7.22, times 100. That's 2.35 percent. On a 50K wire, that's 1,175 dollars my bank kept in the rate. Didn't appear anywhere on the confirmation. I went to World Account next. The actual foreign exchange rate shows on screen before you confirm. No hunting. No Google. Margin sits at 0.3 to 0.5 percent, in plain numbers. Link's in the bio.

Find your last wire confirmation. Locate the applied rate, usually near the bottom. Google the foreign exchange rate for that exact day, that's the mid-market rate. Formula. Interbank minus applied, divided by interbank, times 100. That's your bank's foreign exchange margin in percent. Multiply by wire amount equals what they kept. Applied 7.05, interbank 7.22, that's 2.35 percent. On 50K, 1,175 dollars. World Account quotes the live foreign exchange rate before you hit send, no math, no Google. Margin lands at 0.3 to 0.5 percent. Link's in the bio.

Everyone tells you to check the fee line on your wire confirmation. Truth is, the fee line is a decoy. The real cost is the applied foreign exchange rate, the rate your bank used to convert your money. It's on the confirmation. The mid-market rate, the rate banks use among themselves, is on Google. Subtract, divide, times 100. That's the margin they kept. On most bank wires it's 2 to 3 percent. On 50K, that's 1,000 to 1,500 dollars nobody itemised. World Account shows the live foreign exchange rate before you confirm. Math is done. Link's in the bio.

card_title: Good rate quoted vs real interbank rate

videos.video_title: What 'we'll give you a good rate' actually means

variants.vo: My relationship manager called me last quarter. We'll give you a good rate, she said. I asked what good meant. She said better than retail. So I asked her to text me the rate when the wire goes out. She did. 7.05. I checked Reuters, the global interbank rate at that exact minute, 7.22. That's a 2.4 percent gap. On a 100K wire, that's 2,400 dollars. The rate wasn't bad. It just wasn't what she implied. I moved my sourcing wires to World Account that month. The foreign exchange rate is near-interbank, 0.3 to 0.5 percent off mid-market. No relationship manager needed. The number speaks. Link's in the bio.

Quick maths. Your bank quotes you 7.05 on USD to Chinese yuan. Says it's a good rate. The real interbank rate, the rate banks use among themselves, that minute on Reuters, is 7.22. Gap, 2.4 percent. On 10K, 240 dollars. On 100K, 2,400. Same percent, scales with wire size. World Account quotes near-interbank, the rate is 0.3 to 0.5 percent off the mid-market. On 100K, that's 300 to 500, not 2,400. Difference, 1,900 to 2,100 dollars per wire. Link's in the bio.

Everyone tells you to ask your bank for a good rate. Truth is, when your bank says good rate, they mean good for them. The real foreign exchange rate, the interbank rate banks use among themselves, lives on Reuters. It's free. It's public. The 'good rate' your relationship manager quotes is 2 to 3 percent off that. On a 100K wire, that's 2,000 to 3,000 dollars in their margin. Banks compete on the word 'good'. World Account competes on the number. Near-interbank, 0.3 to 0.5 percent. Link's in the bio.
